papers.title: Low-density and high-modulus steel achieved by hypereutectic TiB2 and high Al ferrite phase
papers.paper_scope: Design and characterization of a novel low-density steel with hypereutectic TiB2 and high-Al δ-ferrite phase to achieve low density, high Young's modulus, and balanced mechanical properties.

processes.description: Vacuum induction melting of 50 kg ingot, homogenization at 1373 K for 2 h, hot rolling in five passes with finishing temperature 1173 K, air cooling to room temperature, producing 3 mm thick sheet.
processes.processes_notes: Hot rolling performed in two-phase region; final sheet thickness 3 mm.

processing_steps.processing_steps_notes: Finishing temperature 1173 K, rolled in two-phase region

structures.overall_structure: Heterogeneous triplex microstructure: equiaxed austenite matrix embedded with elongated δ-ferrite bands and micron-sized TiB2 particles.

phases_present.morphology: irregular particles, primary (hexagonal prisms) and eutectic (plate-like/needle-like)

defects.dislocation_density: high in δ-ferrite near TiB2 interfaces, lower at austenite/TiB2 interfaces

grain_structure.grain_size_distribution: austenite grain size ~3.9 μm (equiaxed), δ-ferrite grain size ~4.4 μm (elongated), δ-ferrite band thickness ~4.3 μm, austenite layer thickness ~12.1 μm

precipitates.distribution: mainly at grain/phase boundaries, avg ~15 μm, coarse >10 μm dominate volume fraction (>80%)

defect_interaction.quality_description: High dislocation density at δ-ferrite/TiB2 interfaces, forming dense dislocation tangles.
Lower dislocation density at austenite/TiB2 interfaces compared to δ-ferrite/TiB2.

interface_notes.stress_strain_distribution: Strain concentration observed at TiB2/matrix interfaces, especially at δ-ferrite/TiB2 and austenite/TiB2 boundaries, leading to strain gradients and GND formation.

tensile_properties.stress_strain_curves: Engineering stress-strain curve presented in Fig. 3(a); true stress-strain curves used in FEM simulations.

service_conditions.stress_state: uniaxial tension, nominal strain rate 1e-3 s^-1

comparative_performance.improvement_percentage: 22% increase in yield strength (503 vs 389 MPa), 30% increase in UTS (672 vs 549 MPa), uniform elongation 13% vs 11%

characterization_methods.purpose: Microstructure observation, particle size and morphology, phase distribution
characterization_methods.key_findings: TiB2 particles size 2.4–35 μm, average ~15 μm; dual-phase matrix with δ-ferrite bands and austenite; area fraction of TiB2 ~9.6% (RD surface), ~13.4% (ND surface).

characterization_methods.purpose: Phase identification, grain orientation, recrystallization fraction, KAM mapping
characterization_methods.key_findings: Area fractions: austenite 59%, δ-ferrite 28%, TiB2 13%. Austenite grains equiaxed, avg size 3.9 μm, 90.8% recrystallized; δ-ferrite elongated, avg size 4.4 μm, 52.2% recrystallized. Higher KAM in δ-ferrite.

Dense dislocation tangles in δ-ferrite near TiB2 interfaces; sparse dislocations in austenite. Coherent orientation relationship at δ-ferrite/TiB2: {110}_δ || {1101}_TiB2, <111>_δ || <1120>_TiB2; no orientation relationship at austenite/TiB2.

characterization_methods.purpose: Quantify dislocation densities of ferrite and austenite using modified Williamson-Hall method
characterization_methods.key_findings: Initial dislocation density: δ-ferrite (1.1±0.2)×10^15 m⁻², austenite (3±0.7)×10^14 m⁻². After tensile fracture: δ-ferrite (1.7±0.7)×10^15 m⁻², austenite (2±0.3)×10^15 m⁻².

characterization_methods.purpose: 3D spatial distribution and size distribution of TiB2 particles
characterization_methods.key_findings: Small particles (0.8–5.0 μm) dominate by number; coarse particles (10.0–42.7 μm) account for >80% of total particle area fraction.

characterization_methods.purpose: Local strain distribution during in-situ tensile testing
characterization_methods.key_findings: Strain concentration at TiB2/matrix interfaces, strain partitioning between austenite and δ-ferrite, higher local strain in softer austenite.

characterization_methods.purpose: Simulate stress-strain partitioning, dislocation density evolution, and damage evolution
characterization_methods.key_findings: Validated against experimental tensile curves and dislocation densities. Phase stress ranking: TiB2 > δ-ferrite > austenite. Heterogeneous microstructure promotes uniform plastic flow and delays shear band localization.

computational_details.simulation_method: Two-dimensional representative volume element (RVE) based on EBSD data, periodic boundary conditions, mesh size 0.4 μm (500×500 elements)
computational_details.validation_against_experiment: Simulated true stress-strain curves and dislocation density evolution closely match experimental results for both present steel and reference Fe-TiB2 steel.

analytical_model.equation_role: Strain hardening in metallic phases (δ-ferrite and austenite)
analytical_model.parameters: k1: 230,000 (δ-ferrite), 200,000 (austenite); k2: 6 (δ-ferrite), 4.5 (austenite); combined with Taylor equation α=0.2, M=3.16/3.06, b=0.248/0.258 nm, μ=80.7/65 GPa
analytical_model.model_note: Dislocation density evolution couples with flow stress via Taylor equation; Hall-Petch strengthening also included.

analytical_model.model_name: Weibull fracture model for TiB2 particles
analytical_model.parameters: σc (critical stress) = 320 MPa, n (shape factor) = 1.5, k (damage scale) = 0.09, f0 = 0.1480, fd0 = 0.1245
analytical_model.model_note: Fracture probability depends on particle volume and internal defects.

analytical_model.equation_role: Interface debonding between matrix and TiB2
analytical_model.parameters: Interface stiffness K = 10^6 MPa mm⁻¹, strength Tc = 700 MPa (δ-ferrite/TiB2), 640 MPa (austenite/TiB2)
analytical_model.model_note: Zero-thickness COH3D8 cohesive elements with bilinear traction-separation law.

unmapped_findings: Specific modulus (E/ρ) of the present steel is 32.7 GPa g⁻¹ cm³, which is higher than typical light metals (Al, Mg, Ti ~25 GPa g⁻¹ cm³).
Specific yield strength (SYS) = 73.1 MPa g⁻¹ cm³, specific ultimate tensile strength (SUTS) = 97.7 MPa g⁻¹ cm³.
δ-ferrite contributes about 170 MPa to the overall flow stress (>30% of yield strength) as a stable strengthening baseline throughout deformation.
The heterogeneous microstructure promotes additional geometrically necessary dislocations (GNDs) due to strain incompatibility between soft austenite and hard δ-ferrite, enhancing strain hardening.
Compared to conventional Fe-TiB2 steel, the present steel exhibits a more uniform dislocation distribution and suppressed shear band formation, leading to improved ductility.
Particle fracture is a major damage mechanism; austenite-surrounded TiB2 particles show higher fracture frequency due to multi-directional shear strains from multiple grains.
Crack tip blunting retards crack propagation from fractured TiB2 particles.
Fractography reveals mixed fracture mode: ductile dimples, quasi-cleavage of TiB2, and particle/matrix decohesion.
The alloy design intentionally avoids carbon to prevent TiC formation and uses B to form TiB2 with superior physical properties.
The steel can be produced via conventional steel metallurgy (induction melting, hot rolling) without requiring advanced processing like splat cooling or additive manufacturing.
